coredata.dc:title: Red pepper waste-derived carbon dots incorporated sodium alginate/gelatin composite films for bioactive fruit preservation

coredata.dc:description: The design of cost-efficient and eco-friendly packaging films with multifunctional features such as antimicrobial, antioxidant, and UV protection is important in food industry. Red pepper waste-derived carbon dots (RP-CDs) have been used as reinforcement and active components in the fabrication of advanced packaging materials. The synthesized spherical RP-CDs with an average diameter of 2.5 nm were rich in active functional groups such as -NH2, -OH, -C=O, CO, and C-O-C. Casting method was used to load 1, 2, and 3 wt% of RP-CDs into sodium alginate/gelatin (SA/Gel) polymer matrix. SEM images and FT-IR and XPS analyses confirmed the strong compatibility and potential hydrogen bonding between RP-CDs and the matrix. This interaction improved the mechanical strength and moisture resistance of the composite films. With the addition of 3 wt% RP-CDs, the UV-A barrier, ABTS radical scavenging, and L. monocytogenes bactericidal properties of the composite film increased by 92.5, 100, and 99.9 %, respectively. Fruit preservation results revealed that the prepared SA/Gel/RP-CD3% composite film maintained the freshness of packed grapes and extended their storage life to 24 days. The development of active packaging films with biowaste-derived functional fillers (CDs) offers the dual benefit of extending the shelf life and contributing to food waste management.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Weight loss is an important external indicator of fruit quality. Fig. 7b shows that all grape samples showed a progressively increasing weight loss rate during the storage period. This is due to the increased rate of water loss caused by respiration and transpiration processes. The weight loss level for grapes of the unpacked sample was remarkably (p < 0.05) more than that of the other experimental groups because of the high-intensity aerobic metabolism and respiration. The low WVP of PE film causes water molecules to accumulate inside, which condense when the temperature changes, promoting microbial growth [62]. The weight loss of grapes wrapped with SA/Gel film was lower than that observed in the PE groups. When RP-CDs were added to the packaging film, the weight loss rate decreased from 10.4 % for SA/Gel film to 4.21 % for SA/Gel/RP-CD3% film, which means that the active filler effectively diminished the weight loss of grapes throughout the experiment. The enhanced structural integrity of the formulated nanocomposite films (supported by SEM images, Fig. 3a,b) can impede the diffusion of gases, moisture, and solutes, thereby reducing metabolic processes and grape respiration.This research was supported by the Brain Pool Program funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT, and Future Planning through the National Research Foundation of Korea (2022H1D3A2A02092788) and the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (RS-2022-NR068166).

This research was supported by the Brain Pool Program funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT, and Future Planning through the National Research Foundation of Korea ( 2022H1D3A2A02092788 ) and the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) ( 2022M3H4A1A03085361 ).

head.ce:title: Red pepper waste-derived carbon dots incorporated sodium alginate/gelatin composite films for bioactive fruit preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of cost-efficient and eco-friendly packaging films with multifunctional features such as antimicrobial, antioxidant, and UV protection is important in food industry. Red pepper waste-derived carbon dots (RP-CDs) have been used as reinforcement and active components in the fabrication of advanced packaging materials. The synthesized spherical RP-CDs with an average diameter of 2.5 nm were rich in active functional groups such as -NH2, -OH, -C=O, CO, and C-O-C. Casting method was used to load 1, 2, and 3 wt% of RP-CDs into sodium alginate/gelatin (SA/Gel) polymer matrix. SEM images and FT-IR and XPS analyses confirmed the strong compatibility and potential hydrogen bonding between RP-CDs and the matrix. This interaction improved the mechanical strength and moisture resistance of the composite films. With the addition of 3 wt% RP-CDs, the UV-A barrier, ABTS radical scavenging, and L. monocytogenes bactericidal properties of the composite film increased by 92.5, 100, and 99.9 %, respectively. Fruit preservation results revealed that the prepared SA/Gel/RP-CD3% composite film maintained the freshness of packed grapes and extended their storage life to 24 days. The development of active packaging films with biowaste-derived functional fillers (CDs) offers the dual benefit of extending the shelf life and contributing to food waste management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Pepper (Capsicum annum L.) is an important crop worldwide, valued both as a food and as a favorite vegetable for many people [1]. In addition to being a rich source of ascorbic acid, it provides significant amounts of carotenoids, vitamins, nitrogenous compounds, phenols, saponins, flavonoids, and minerals. Therefore, pepper is known for its notable health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, anticancer, antioxidant, anti-arthritic, and antifungal properties. However, it is a highly perishable fruit due to over 80 % water content and usually faces problems such as quality deterioration, color alteration, and fast weight loss after harvest [2]. It has been reported that around 46 % of capsicum crops are lost each year at different stages of the supply chain, including cultivation, transportation, storage, distribution, and consumption [1]. Large amounts of pepper waste can pose significant economic, environmental, and food security problems. Improper disposal methods, such as landfilling and open dumping, can result in environmental pollution, including greenhouse gas emissions and soil contamination. Food waste in landfills is estimated to be the third largest source of greenhouse gas emission, generating approximately 4.4 billion tons of greenhouse gases each year. The significant environmental impact, combined with the limited availability of disposal sites and high waste transportation costs, has prompted the adoption of more efficient, environmentally friendly, and sustainable waste management processes [2].

Repurposing discarded fruits and vegetables can yield positive outcomes, including contributing to environmental conservation, enhancing food security, and promoting economic development. There is a growing trend in the adoption of innovative technologies and methods focused on food waste recycling. One effective approach is the sustainable utilization of food waste to create value-added functional materials [3,4]. Green fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) from biomass and renewable natural resources has garnered notable interest because of their distinctive attributes, including easy preparation, cost-effectiveness, high biocompatibility, and the potential for large-scale manufacturing [5]. CDs derived from food waste hold promising potential for enhancing food quality assessment and safety detection measures. Various types of waste, encompassing seeds, grains, leaves, fruits, coffee, milk, and tea, have been explored as eco-friendly precursors for the production of CDs [6]. Food-based CDs have been successfully applied in drug delivery, tissue engineering, biological imaging, phototherapy, cancer treatment, and food packaging applications. The demand for CDs as an active component in the food packaging sector has been steadily increasing [7]. CDs are composed of an sp2-conjugated amorphous or nanocrystalline core with oxygenated surface functional moieties (e.g., -OH, -COOH and -CHO) [8]. These edges side functional moieties of CDs impart bioactivity, providing additional functionality to packaging materials and maintaining the freshness and quality of food [7]. Fresh fruits and vegetables, in particular, are prone to contamination with microbes and are challenging to package due to their high moisture level and continuous biological action after harvest [9]. Several investigations have verified that loading CDs into packaging materials can extend the storage life of fruits and vegetables [10–12].

The polymeric matrix can be employed as a carrier to mix with these functional components, fabricating the environmentally friendly multifunctional active films [13]. At the end of the life cycle of plastics derived from fossil fuels, the adoption of biopolymers as active packaging has been recommended. Food-safe film-forming biopolymers, including agar, chitosan, alginate, cellulose, starch, pectin, and carrageenan, were employed to fabricate edible films and composite films [14]. Gelatin is a natural protein-based biopolymer composed of randomly arranged polypeptide chains and is odorless when dissolved in water [15]. The potential use of gelatin as a packaging material is attributed to its triple-helix structure, which offers better strength and UV absorption capability. This helps slow down chemical oxidation reactions in food [16]. Nevertheless, films made from gelatin showed some limitations, such as weak mechanical and water resistance attributes [17]. The blending of gelatin with various polysaccharides has been proposed to reduce these drawbacks of gelatin [18]. On the other hand, sodium alginate (SA) is a linear anionic polysaccharide isolated from the brown algae's cell walls. The gelation capacity of alginate can promote the cohesiveness, rigidity, strength, and gas barrier properties of films. The interaction between SA and Gel leads to the formation of a network-like structure, resulting in stable films with excellent flexibility, durability, and low gas permeability [19]. Sodium alginate can interact with gelatin through electrostatic interactions between the carboxyl groups of alginate and the amino groups of gelatin. Additionally, hydrogen bonding and physical entanglements contribute to the formation of a stable polymer network [20]. These interactions enhance the mechanical strength and structural integrity of the composite film.

In this study, red pepper waste was used as a sustainable source to fabricate multifunctional CDs for active food packaging applications via a simple economic hydrothermal method. By utilizing food waste as a valuable resource, red pepper waste was successfully converted into eco-friendly and multifunctional fluorescent CDs. This strategy not only provides an effective solution for recycling food waste but also addresses waste treatment challenges, promotes circular economy, and facilitates the development of functional hybrid nanomaterials for food packaging applications. The prepared red pepper waste-derived carbon dots (RP-CDs) were examined in the aspects of chemical, structural morphology, and functional properties. They were then incorporated into an alginate/gelatin (SA/Gel) matrix to design a new sustainable active packaging system. The physicochemical properties of SA/Gel/RP-CD composite films were thoroughly investigated. Moreover, the effect of RP-CD addition on the gas barrier, surface wettability, UV blocking, mechanical, antibacterial, and antioxidant attributes was assessed. The effectiveness of SA/Gel/RP-CDs composite films in the preservation of harvested fruits was assessed by monitoring the quality parameters of grapes (Vitis vinifera) during the storage period.

The food-grade gelatin (Type A from fish skin, 200 Bloom) was obtained from Gel-Tec Co., Ltd. (Seoul, Korea), and sodium alginate (SA) with a viscosity of 5.0–40.0 cps (1 % solution at 25 °C) was bought from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 13565), Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 15313), Escherichia coli O157: H7 (ATCC 43895), and Salmonella enterica (ATCC 14028) were procured from the Korean Collection for Type Culture (KCTC). Aspergillus flavus (ATCC 22546) and Penicillium chrysogenum (ATCC 22959) were provided by the Korean Culture Center of Microorganisms (KCCM). The red pepper and fresh table grapes were bought from a local supermarket in Seoul, Korea.

To synthesize red pepper waste-derived carbon dots (RP-CDs), waste red pepper flakes were first collected and thoroughly washed with distilled water. The washed flakes were then dried in an oven at 60 °C. After drying, the sample was finely ground using a laboratory-scale blender (Green Mix, model DA700-G). RP-CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method as shown in Fig. 1 . For this purpose, 1 g of the dried red pepper powder was dispersed in 50 mL of distilled water and sonicated for 15 min using a probe-type ultrasonic processor (VCX750, Sonics and Materials Inc., Newtown, CT, USA). The resulting dispersion was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 190 °C for 8 h. After cooling, the synthesized carbon dots were separated by centrifugation of the reaction mixture at 10,000 rpm for 20 min using a benchtop centrifuge (Hanil Scientific Centrifuge, Incheon, Gyeonggi-do, Korea) to obtain the final RP-CD solution. After centrifugation, the supernatant was carefully collected and filtered through a Whatman membrane filter (25 mm diameter, 0.22 μm nylon filter) to remove residual impurities. The filtered supernatant was collected as RP-CD solution. The yield of RP-CDs in aqueous solution was determined to be approximately 5.3 mg/mL. Finally, the RP-CD solution was stored at 4 °C until further analysis.

The sodium alginate/gelatin (SA/Gel)-based composite films were prepared using the casting technique (Fig. 1). Film casting solution was prepared by dispersing 2 g of sodium alginate powder in 100 mL distilled water and heating at 90 °C for 1 h. Separately, gelatin at a concentration of 2 % (w/v) was dispersed in 100 mL distilled water while stirred at 80 °C for 1 h. After the complete dissolution of SA and Gel, the polymer solutions were blended in a 1:1 ratio in the presence of glycerol (30 %, w/w, based on polymer) by stirring for 1 h at 70 °C. Subsequently, to incorporate the CDs as functional filler, different amounts of RP-CDs (1, 2, and 3 wt%, based on SA/Gel) were added into the above SA/Gel solutions under constant stirring for 30 min at 55 °C. The solutions were poured onto a rectangular Teflon-coated glass plate (30 cm × 24 cm) and left for 72 h at 25 °C. The peeled-off films were kept at 25 °C/50 % RH (relative humidity) chamber for at least 72 h before analysis. The pure sodium alginate/gelatin film was designed as SA/Gel. The composite film samples were coded based on the RP-CD concentrations as SA/Gel/RP-CD1%, SA/Gel/RP-CD2%, and SA/Gel/RP-CD3%, respectively.
Comprehensive information regarding the characterization methods of the fabricated RP-CDs and SA/Gel-based films is presented in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the composite films on fruit preservation
ce:section.ce:para: Table grapes (Vitis vinifera) are a widely consumed fruit globally, valued for their excellent taste and high content of bioactive compounds such as vitamins and flavonoids. The global annual production of table grapes exceeds 27 million tons, with around 4.2 million tons exported internationally [3]. However, multiple factors such as bunch dehydration, alterations in peel color, rachis browning, and colonization by different spoilage fungi cause substantial fruit waste and economic losses during exportation. As a result, there is a growing focus on expanding the storage life of table grapes to meet the demands of the export markets [21]. Therefore, table grapes were used as a food model to evaluate whether the designed SA/Gel/RP-CD composite film could be practically applied as a bioactive packaging material to extend food shelf life.

Fresh grapes (three replicate samples) were wrapped in i) unpacked and commercial polyethylene film as control and ii) 10 cm × 10 cm testing sterile films (SA/Gel, SA/Gel/RP-CD1%, SA/Gel/RP-CD2%, and SA/Gel/RP-CD3%). The packages were stored at 4 °C for 24 days, and changes in fruit quality were monitored every 8 days by recording weight loss, hardness, pH, and bacterial growth of the samples. The initial weight of the fruits was measured using a precision balance, and the subsequent weights were recorded at 8-day intervals. The mass loss rate was determined as the percentage of weight loss relative to the initial weight. The hardness of the treated samples was determined using a fruit hardness tester (FR5105, Lutron, Hanoi, Vietnam) equipped with a 30 mm cylindrical probe. The probe penetrated the samples at a constant test speed of 1 mm/s. The maximum puncture force (N) was recorded and used to express the hardness of the samples. Five grams of grape sample were added to 45 mL of 0.1 % peptone water and digested using a stomacher (BagMixer 400, Interscience, Saint Nom la Bretêche, France). The pH of the homogenized samples was measured using a calibrated digital pH meter. In addition, 100 μL of serially diluted smashed grape juice was evenly spread on agar plates to quantify the bacterial population in the stored fruits. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 48 h. The total bacterial cell count (TBC) was then determined by counting the colony-forming units (CFU) present on the agar plates. Each analysis was repeated at least three times, and the results were expressed as mean values.

Each analysis was repeated at least three times, and the outcomes were presented as mean ± SD (standard deviation). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed, and the significance of each mean attribute value was evaluated (p < 0.05) through Duncan's multiple range test using Statistical Analysis (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) Software.

The morphology and particle size of the prepared RP-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM image illustrates the successful formation of RP-CD nanodots with a spherical shape (Fig. 2a). High-resolution TEM shows that RP-CDs have a lattice structure with a spacing distance of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon (sp2) (Fig. 2b). This suggests that RP-CDs have a graphite-like structure. Additionally, the particle size distribution histogram indicates a narrow size range of RP-CDs with an average size of about 2.5 nm, exhibiting the homogeneous and well-dispersed nature of the prepared RP-CDs (Fig. 2c). Furthermore, the stability of the aqueous solution of RP-CDs was confirmed by determining the zeta potential analysis. The zeta potential of the proposed RP-CDs was measured to be −19.58 mV. Previous studies have shown that a zeta potential value of higher than ±15 mV indicates good stability for the aqueous solution of CDs [22].

UV–visible and photoluminescence (FL) spectroscopy were used to investigate the optical properties of the synthesized RP-CDs (Fig. 2d & e). The surface plasmon resonance (SPR) spectrum of the RP-CD solution showed a distinct absorption peak around 282 nm. This peak is attributed to the n-π* transition of -C=O/C − OH groups and the π-π* transition of -C=C, which confirms the successful formation of CDs (Fig. 2d) [23,24]. In contrast, no distinct SPR peak was observed for the aqueous dispersion of red pepper powder (Fig. S1a, supporting information). This could be due to the large size of the uncarbonized particles, which prevented proper absorption of UV radiation in the 280 to 300 nm range.

Additionally, the strongest emission wavelength is recorded at 452 nm, which corresponds to an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The inset demonstrates that the yellowish RP-CDs emit bright blue FL when exposed to 365 nm UV light. The recorded fluorescence spectra of RP-CDs at various excitation wavelengths depicted an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior (Fig. 2e). Changing the wavelength from 280 to 500 nm induced a red shift in the FL emission peak, attributed to the inhomogeneous particle size and surface defects [25].

Fig. 3a shows the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of RP-CDs, exploring the chemical structure and surface functionality of RP-CDs. A strong band at 3442 to 3020cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of -OH and -NH groups [23]. Due to the highly hydrophilic nature of CDs, the broad peak of -OH may mask the presence of the -NH peak (~3100 cm−1), resulting in the observation of only a single broad peak. However, to confirm the presence of nitrogen element, a small peak observed around ~2200 cm−1 indicates the existence of the nitrile group. The IR peak around 1550 cm−1 indicates the presence of the amino group in the RP-CDs [23]. The vibration peak at 2923 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration mode of the -C-H bond. The absorption peaks centered at 1770, 1585, and 1191cm−1 represent the vibrations of the oxygen-containing carbon core -C=O, CO, and C-O-C. The characteristic peaks at 1285, 1388, and 1110cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibrations of CN, CC, and C–NH–C bonds, respectively [26]. The stretching vibration peak at 779cm−1 is related to the characteristic absorption peak of CS [27]. Remarkably, a substantial number of carboxyl, hydroxyl or amino groups are anchored on the RP-CD surface, resulting in strong hydrophilicity, good dispersibility, and functional properties in the RP-CDs [28].

The FTIR spectrum of red pepper powder (Fig. S1b, supporting information) showed similar characteristic peaks corresponding to groups such as -OH/NH, -COOH, and -CO, among others. The similar peaks observed for both RP-CDs and red pepper powder resulted from the overlapping vibrational stretching of -OH, -N-H, CH, CC, and -CO groups in nearly the same IR region. However, the sharp absorption peaks appearing with a slight shift in the IR spectrum of RP-CDs confirms the presence of abundant free functional sites on the CDs surface.

Free radicals can initiate chain reactions that result in the degradation of lipids, proteins, and other sensitive components in food. Antioxidants act as radical scavengers by capturing and neutralizing these free radicals, thereby reducing the oxidative processes that cause deterioration and spoilage [29]. The antioxidant performance of the synthesized RP-CDs was evaluated through the ABTS/DPPH radicals scavenging assays (Fig. 3b). The results showed an enhanced radical scavenging ability of RP-CDs with the increasing concentration from 0 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL, revealing a concentration-dependent behavior of RP-CDs. The free radical neutralizing power of the RP-CDs at the concentration of 12.5 μg/mL was 14.5 ± 0.8 % and 23.3 ± 1.1 % against DPPH and ABTS. At a concentration of 100 μg/mL, the neutralizing efficiency of RP-CDs reached 100 % for both DPPH and ABTS. The antioxidant activity index (AAI) was calculated to determine the antioxidant power of RP-CDs. The AAI value of RP-CDs was 0.76 and 3.25 for DPPH and ABTS methods. Generally, AAI values <0.5 specify weak antioxidant activity, AAI values of 0.5 to 1 indicate moderate antioxidant action, AAI values of 1 to 2 are considered strong antioxidant activity, and AAI values >2 express very strong antioxidant performance [30]. Therefore, the proposed RP-CDs can be considered as a moderate antioxidant against DPPH and a very strong antioxidant towards ABTS. The higher antioxidant action of RP-CDs against ABTS compared to the DPPH can be due to the hydrophilic characteristic of CDs, which facilitate the dispersion of RP-CDs in ABTS (aqueous) than in methanol-based DPPH solution [11].

A possible free radical scavenging mechanism of RP-CDs is the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the RP-CDs surface. These groups act as proton donors to transfer the unpaired electrons of radicals to the carbon core containing the CC backbone structure [31]. This proton transfer quenches the free radicals into stable complexes, which change the color of ABTS/DPPH solutions from cyan/purple to colorless/yellow, respectively. In addition, the electron donation of RP-CDs can quench free radicals through adduct formation, delocalization, and stabilization of free electrons in the sp2 domains of RP-CDs [10,32].

Bacterial contamination is a primary factor in the spoilage of many foods. Antibacterial packaging can inhibit the proliferation of microorganisms and preserve the quality and organoleptic characteristics of food [33,34]. Agar well diffusion assay and MIC/MBC assessment were used to determine the antibacterial action of RP-CDs solution towards common pathogenic bacteria (Fig. 3c). The MIC/MBC values of RP-CDs for S. enterica and E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) were 300/400 and 400/500 μg/mL, while those for S. aureus and L. monocytogenes (Gram-positive bacteria) were 200/300 and 100/200 μg/mL, respectively. The bacteriostatic zones of 3.9 ± 0.2 mm (E. coli), 4.5 ± 0.3 mm (S. enterica), 6.8 ± 0.50 mm (L. monocytogenes), and 5.1 ± 0.40 mm (S. aureus) were observed after 24 h of incubation. These results demonstrate the strong antibacterial activity of the prepared RP-CDs, aligning with findings from previous studies [25,35,36].

Additionally, the antifungal ability of RP-CDs was evaluated against two fungal strains (A. flavus and P. chrysogenum) (Fig. 3d-g). Control agar plates without any functional additive (Fig. 3d,f) were completely covered with A. flavus and P. chrysogenum fungal strains. However, fungal growth was notably inhibited on agar plates containing RP-CD particles (Fig. 3e,g), suggesting the antifungal action of prepared RP-CDs.

RP-CDs possibly inactivate microorganisms through the following antimicrobial mechanisms: Zero-dimensional carbon nanodots with high surface area can attach to the cell walls of bacteria and fungi through diffusion and electrostatic action, preventing their nutrient uptake [37]. The absorbed carbon dots can form non-covalent bonds with bacterial DNA and RNA, thereby changing the secondary structure of DNA and RNA and affecting the genetic processes of bacteria. The adsorbed CDs can penetrate the cell membrane, damaging the bacterial cell wall and leading to cytoplasmic leakage [38]. Furthermore, the CDs induce ROS production in the intracellular space of bacterial cells. The generated ROS, such as HO• and O2 •− or 1O2 (singlet molecular oxygen), disrupt the oxidative balance, damaging vital biomolecules and ultimately leading to cell death [39].

The intermolecular interactions between RP-CDs and polymer background were evaluated by FT-IF spectroscopy (Fig. 4a). For pure SA/Gel film, the absorption peaks at 3272, 2931, 2872, and 1026 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibrations of OH, CH, -CH2, and C-O-C bonds, respectively. The stretching vibrations of CO (amide I), the bending vibrations of NH (amide II), and the CN plane and NH stretching vibrations (amide III) were associated with characteristic peaks at 1604, 1545, and 1235 cm−1, respectively [40,41]. SA/Gel-based composite films containing different amounts of RP-CDs exhibited similar spectra with the pure SA/Gel film despite the variation in peak intensity. The splitting of the amide I peak into two distinct peaks at approximately 1607 and 1625 cm−1, along with additional peak shifts, confirms the successful incorporation of RP-CDs into the SA/Gel polymer. This result implies electrostatic interactions and the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonding between RP-CDs and SA/Gel polymer backbone [42]. This finding is consistent with the reports on active packaging films containing various biopolymers and carbon dot fillers [32,38,43].

XPS spectroscopy is further employed to confirm the composition and chemical structure of designed SA/Gel/RP-CDs films. The elemental analysis results reveal that SA/Gel mainly consists of carbon (C 1 s, 64.83 %), oxygen (O 1 s, 23.42 %) and nitrogen (N 1 s, 11.75 %) elements (Fig. 4b). However, the presence of an excess sulfur peak (S 2p, 0.6 %) for SA/Gel/RP-CD3% indicates that the polymer matrix contains active functional groups of RP-CDs. In detail, the C 1 s spectra of SA/Gel and SA/Gel/RP-CD3% contain C=C/C-C (283.4), CN (285.1), CO (286.7), and N-C=O (287.6) bands, respectively (Fig. 4c). The spectrum of O 1 s in Fig. 4d can be attributed to O=C-O, C-O-C/C-O-H, and O=C-O-C bonds, which are assigned to binding energies of 530.3, 531.3, and 532.8 eV, respectively [44]. The N 1 s XPS spectra of both films (Fig. 4e) show three characteristic peaks at 398.4, 398.9, and 399.6 eV, which are related to the CN, CN, and NH bonds, respectively. The addition of 3 wt% RP-CDs to the polymer enhanced the peak intensity related to the NH bond, confirming the presence of relative nitrogen species of RP-CDs in the SA/Gel/RP-CD film samples. Furthermore, S 2p mainly consists of peaks at 164.5, 166.1, and 167.5 eV, indicating C-S-O, -OSO3 2− (S 2P3/2), and -OSO3 2− (S 2P1/2) groups, respectively (Fig. 4f) [45]. The findings indicated that the abundant surface functional groups of RP-CDs can contribute to the enhanced stability of the composite films through the formation of stable hydrogen bonds with SA/Gel [46].

The microstructural properties of SA/Gel-based films were monitored through the FE-SEM analysis, and the images are presented in Fig. 5 (a & b). The top surface SEM micrograph of the SA/Gel control film (Fig. 5a) demonstrated a homogeneous morphology without any cracks or voids, exhibiting good mixing and compatibility between alginate and gelatin macromolecules. The SA/Gel film integrated with 1, 2, and 3 wt% of RP-CDs showed uniform microstructure without particles aggregating, indicating that the RP-CDs in the polymer were uniformly distributed [47]. The enhanced integrity and smoothness of SA/Gel/RP-CDs composite films suggest good interaction and compatibility between the active filler and polymer matrix, which may play an important role in the promotion of physicochemical properties of SA/Gel-based films. Besides, all SA/Gel-based films revealed a smooth and homogeneous cross-sectional morphology (Fig. 5b). However, possible hydrogen bonding between the functional groups of RP-CDs and the polymer backbone resulted in a more continuous and compact structure [48].

The elemental composition of the formulated films was analyzed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and elemental mapping techniques (Fig. S4, Supporting information). EDX analysis of the pure SA/Gel film revealed the presence of only carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) elements. In contrast, the detection of sulfur (S) element in the EDX spectra of SA/Gel/RP-CDs affirmed the effective integration of RP-CDs filler into the polymer matrix. Furthermore, elemental mapping of the composite films confirmed the uniform distribution of sulfur elements, indicating that the RP-CD particles were well dispersed in the biopolymer matrix.

The color of the packaging surface directly impacts the visual presentation of the food item and influences consumer preferences [49]. Colorimetric parameters, including lightness (L), redness (a), yellowness (b & YI), and whiteness (WI), were recorded to estimate the color of the composite films (Table 1 ). The SA/Gel control film illustrated the highest L value (89.5 ± 0.1), indicating the colorless and glossy appearance of the film. After adding various concentrations of RP-CDs, the L value of the composite films decreased, indicating a decrease in the brightness of the composite films. The a value of SA/Gel-based film significantly (p < 0.05) decreased with the increase of RP-CD concentration, while the b value remarkably (p < 0.05) increased depending on the RP-CD concentration. As a result, the WI of films decreased from 87.6 for SA/Gel to 60.2 for SA/Gel/RP-CD3% film, and the YI of films increased from 8.9 for SA/Gel to 61.6 for SA/Gel/RP-CD3% film. This is due to the yellow color of RP-CDs. Overall, the fabricated SA/Gel/RP-CD3% composite film demonstrated high transparency, as confirmed by its L value of 80.9 ± 0.1 and WI value of 60.2 ± 0.4. Therefore, the quantitative determination of color parameters validated the suitability of the proposed composite films for food packaging purposes (Table 1). The ΔE values of SA/Gel-based films significantly (p < 0.05) increased with increasing filler concentration which resulted in a noticeable color change in the films that was visible to the naked eye when the ΔE value exceeded 3 [50].

UV light significantly contributes to the oxidation of food. Hence, the UV barrier action of packaging film can enhance the overall life storage of food products and thereby reduce economic losses [48]. The UV–Vis light transmittance of prepared samples is depicted in Fig. 5d. The SA/Gel control film showed low transmittance in the UV region, which corresponds to the characteristics phenomenon of the π-π* transition of the CO group in the biopolymers. Moreover, the high transmittance in the visible region expresses the high transparency of fabricated SA/Gel control film. The addition of RP-CDs declined the UV-light transmittance depending on the concentration without disturbing the transparency of the films. The UV-absorption characteristic of RP-CDs imparts UV-blocking action to the developed composite film (Fig. 2d). The transparency (T660) and the UV-shielding ability (T280) of the films are presented in Table 1. The pure SA/Gel film transmitted 84.5 % of visible light at 660 nm (T660). The transmission of light in films in the visible region (T660) didn't change statistically by the incorporation of 1, 2, and 3 wt% RP-CDs, confirming the substantial transparency of fabricated nanocomposite films. However, the transmission of UV in the films decreased dramatically from 9.3 % for SA/Gel film to 0.03 % for SA/Gel/RP-CD3% film, which determined the UV-shielding characteristics of the proposed composite films. The power to block UVC, UVB, and UVA was investigated by assessing light transmission at 200–400 nm (Fig. 5e). The pure SA/Gel film transmitted 46.19 % UVA light. The integration of RP-CDs induced excellent protecting capacity against UVA, exhibiting 92.5 % UVA-blocking action for the SA/Gel/RP-CD3% film. This attribute is notably beneficial due to the fact that UVA light is most penetrative and is closely linked to photocatalytic reactions that can deteriorate food quality [51]. Moreover, the SA/Gel/RP-CD composite films demonstrated superior UVB shielding capacity by obtaining 99.9 % UVB blocking ability for SA/Gel/RP-CD3% film. The outcome presents that the engineered SA/Gel/RP-CD films not only exhibit impressive UV-light shielding effects but also fulfill consumers' aesthetic preferences for food items.

Adequate mechanical strength is crucial to maintain their capacity to endure stress throughout storage, shipping, and sales. This minimizes the risk of packaging failure and protects food against contamination [52]. The thickness and mechanical aspects of the packaging film are evaluated by measuring the tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EB), and elastic modulus (EM) (Table 2 ). The thickness of the pure SA/Gel film (48.7 ± 1.2 μm) significantly increased (p < 0.05) with the addition of RP-CDs, reaching 55.5 ± 1.3 μm for the SA/Gel/RP-CD3% composite film. This increase was attributed to the enhanced solid content in the polymer matrix [53]. However, no significant differences were observed between various RP-CD concentrations.

The pristine SA/Gel film exhibited the lowest TS of 59.8 MPa and EM of 2391.7 MPa. The addition of RP-CDs to the SA/Gel polymer films presented a significant (P < 0.05) increase in the TS and EM. The addition of 3 wt% RP-CDs enhanced the strength and stiffness of the pure film by 53.1 % and 29.6 %, respectively. Zero-dimensional RP-CDs have high specific surface area and functional groups, which can enhance the cross-linking and intermolecular interactions with the SA/Gel polymer backbone. This enhances the stress transfer from the polymer matrix to the RP-CD fillers [54]. Moreover, EB decreased with increasing RP-CD content, consistent with reported data [39,51]. The abundant hydrogen bonds formed between the fillers and the biopolymer matrix may hinder the mobility of the biopolymer network, decreasing the films' flexibility. Therefore, the SA/Gel/RP-CD composite films presented notably better strength while retaining adequate stiffness and flexibility.

Water resistance is an essential attribute of a food packaging system to protect the package against moisture and maintain the cohesiveness of the packaging substance [55]. Water contact angle (WCA) is frequently employed to evaluate surface water resistance and serve as a marker of the surface's hydrophilicity. The pure SA/Gel film presented a WCA of 62.8°, and the addition of 1 wt% RP-CDs increased the WC to 70.9° (Table 2). The uniform distribution of RP-CDs within the matrix promotes the formation of -OH bonds between the RP-CDs and biopolymers. This results in physical cross-linking, effectively reducing the exposure of hydrophilic groups in the films [56]. After adding 2 and 3 wt% RP-CDs, the WCA decreased significantly (p < 0.05) and reached 68.9 and 64.4. However, the WCA of the SA/Gel/RP-CD films remained higher than that of the pure SA/Gel film due to the synergistic effect of increased surface roughness and physical cross-linking from the integration of RP-CDs. The results showed that the hybridization of RP-CDs with SA/Gel polymer matrix decreased the water affinity of the packaging film.

Effective gas barrier action of packaging materials can prolong food shelf life by retarding the diffusion of water vapor between the outside and inside of the package [57]. The WVP of pure SA/Gel film was 12.1 ± 0.3 g.m/m2.Pa.s (Table 2). The WVP of SA/Gel/RP-CDs films declined notably (p < 0.05) at low RP-CDs amount (1 wt%) but enhanced insignificantly (p > 0.05) at high RP-CDs concentration (2 and 3 wt%). The initial WVP reduction is attributed to the establishment of a tortuous diffusion route for water vapor caused by a cross-linked network of RP-CDs and SA/Gel [58]. Moreover, the enhanced intermolecular interactions between RP-CDs and polymer form a compact film structure, which improves the moisture barrier of the designed films [51]. The increase in WVP is attributed to the polar nature of RP-CDs containing abundant free hydroxyl groups. These groups can create additional water vapor pathways within the microstructure of the film, thereby reducing the barrier properties against water vapor [59]. The results suggested that the addition of RP-CDs improved the water resistance and gas barrier properties of pure SA/Gel film, indicating that they have great potential for use as packaging materials.

In addition, the water solubility and swelling degree of composite films were discovered to be less than pure SA/Gel film (Table S1, Supporting information). The observed results are attributed to the physical cross-linking and the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between RP-CDs and the SA/Gel polymer backbone. These interactions form a compact film structure that limits the space available to entrap moisture, improving the water resistance of the films [7].

The bactericidal power of the composite films was measured against two prevalent types of pathogenic and spoilage bacteria in food (L. monocytogenes and E. coli). Fig. 6a illustrates the capacity of all samples to inhibit the growth of the tested bacteria. The pristine SA/Gel film showed no antibacterial effect compared to the control due to the acting as a nutrient source for bacteria. Loading RP-CDs into the SA/Gel film notably (p < 0.05) improved the inhibitory effect of the films. The bacterial growth on agar plates for different film samples confirms the concentration-dependent antibacterial properties of the composite films. The SA/Gel/RP-CD3% film exhibited strong antibacterial activity, with a 100 % growth inhibition against L. monocytogenes and 99 % inhibition against E. coli (Fig. 6b). The released RP-CDs possibly exhibited a synergistic microbial inactivation effect through (1) disruption of the integrity of bacterial cell membranes, (2) penetration into bacterial cells and interruption of DNA structure, and (3) heightened production of ROS in bacterial cells [60]. However, it is important to highlight that the antibacterial effectiveness of all active films was notably greater towards L. monocytogenes compared to E. coli. This may be related to differences in the structural arrangement of the bacterial cell membrane. The outer membrane in Gram-positive bacteria exhibits higher permeability than Gram-negative bacteria [33].

The radical neutralization performance of the SA/Gel-based films was assessed using the ABTS and DPPH radical neutralizing technique (Fig. 6c). All film samples presented a concentration-dependent antiradical scavenging action. The pure SA/Gel film showed radical scavenging rates of up to 17.1 % for DPPH and up to 19.3 % for ABTS at a film amount of 45 mg. This is attributed to the specific amino acids in gelatin, such as glycine and proline. The antioxidant efficiency of SA/Gel/RP-CDs films enhanced notably (p < 0.05) as the RP-CDS amount increased. The SA/Gel/RP-CD3% film induced nearly 100 % ABTS and 65.4 % DPPH radical neutralization abilities for 45 mg film samples in a 10 mL radical solution. These enhancements can be credited to the existence of surface-reducible electron-donating moieties in the RP-CDs and the proton-transferring ability of CDs. As a result, the proposed composite films can prevent food oxidation by scavenging free radicals. This enhances the safety and stability of packaged foods by reducing the formation of harmful compounds, such as lipid peroxides, which are commonly generated during the oxidation process.

Additionally, a migration test of RP-CDs into various food simulant solutions (water, 50 % ethanol, and 4 % acetic acid) was conducted to evaluate the efficiency of the films in contact with food (Fig. S5, supporting information). The results showed the concentration of RP-CDs and the type of food simulant significantly influenced the release rate and the highest release was observed in water for SA/Gel films containing 3 % RP-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of formulated SA/Gel-based films for fruit preservation
ce:section.ce:para: Table grapes were used as a model food to evaluate the suitability of the designed SA/Gel/RP-CDs active film for freshness preservation of perishable foods. Post-harvest processing, storage, and marketing of table grapes pose serious problems for this popular fruit. Deterioration of grapes is mainly caused by mold growth, which reduces the marketability of the product due to softening, weight loss, and color changes [61]. Antimicrobial packaging is recommended to extend the shelf life of grapes from storage to domestic use. The quality and acceptability of stored fruits were evaluated based on overall visual aspects, weight loss, texture, pH, and total bacteria count (TBC) (Fig. 7 ). Digital photographs of packed grapes depicted the different decay rates of samples through different treatments (Fig. 7a). The degradation rate of grapes, indicated by visible deterioration and mold spot formation, increased rapidly in those stored without packaging, or in PE and SA/Gel-based packaging groups. However, the degradation degree of grapes treated with SA/Gel/RP-CD composite films decreased depending on the RP-CD concentration, confirming the acceptable preservation performance of designed films.

Weight loss is an important external indicator of fruit quality. Fig. 7b shows that all grape samples showed a progressively increasing weight loss rate during the storage period. This is due to the increased rate of water loss caused by respiration and transpiration processes. The weight loss level for grapes of the unpacked sample was remarkably (p < 0.05) more than that of the other experimental groups because of the high-intensity aerobic metabolism and respiration. The low WVP of PE film causes water molecules to accumulate inside, which condense when the temperature changes, promoting microbial growth [62]. The weight loss of grapes wrapped with SA/Gel film was lower than that observed in the PE groups. When RP-CDs were added to the packaging film, the weight loss rate decreased from 10.4 % for SA/Gel film to 4.21 % for SA/Gel/RP-CD3% film, which means that the active filler effectively diminished the weight loss of grapes throughout the experiment. The enhanced structural integrity of the formulated nanocomposite films (supported by SEM images, Fig. 3a,b) can impede the diffusion of gases, moisture, and solutes, thereby reducing metabolic processes and grape respiration.

Firmness acts as another crucial parameter in assessing the quality degradation of fruit. As depicted in Fig. 7c, the hardness diminished across all treat groups with increasing storage time. However, the most substantial reduction was obtained in the unpacked and PE groups. The firmness decreased from 3.6 ± 0.1 to 1.5 ± 0.1, 0.98 ± 0.1, and 0.71 ± 0.2 N for the unpacked, PE, and SA/Gel groups, respectively. Firmness drastically decreases during storage because of microbiological contamination and cell structure deterioration induced by cell wall degrading enzymes [63]. On the other hand, the hardness in the SA/Gel/RP-CD1% and SA/Gel/RP-CD2% groups treatment still retained the values of 1.32 ± 0.1 and 1.70 ± 0.1 N, respectively, even after 24 days of storing. The hardest grape was obtained in the SA/Gel/RP-CD3% film group (2.2 ± 0.1 N), suggesting that storage-induced physicochemical changes in grapes were obtained by packaging containing RP-CDs.

The pH of fresh grapes was 4.19 ± 0.10, which increased with a longer storage period depending on packaging type (Fig. 7d). Enzymatic activity and natural aging during storage can damage cell membranes, resulting in a decrease in acid content and a corresponding increase in pH [64]. The pH values of grapes subjected to various treatments were determined as unpacked ∼ PE ∼ SA/Gel > SA/Gel/RP-CDs, suggesting that fruits covered with composite films can maintain pH at a good edible value.

Fig. 7e shows the alteration in the total bacterial count (TBC) of grapes over the storage period. TBC tended to rise across all groups with the extension of the storage period. The TBC in fresh grapes was 1.97 log CFU/g. After 24 days of storage, the TBC increased to 3.5, 7.9, and 7.47 log CFU/g in unpacked, PE, and SA/Gel samples, respectively. However, the TBC decreased to 5.5 and 4 log CFU/g for SA/Gel/RP-CD1% and SA/Gel/RP-CD2% film groups, and the lowest TBC of 3.3 log CFU/g was observed in the SA/Gel/RP-CD3% group samples. These results demonstrate the significant antimicrobial effect of RP-CDs in reducing the number of microorganisms in grapes over 24 days of storage. This confirms the efficacy of the fabricated bioactive SA/Gel/RP-CDs film in inhibiting food-borne pathogens. Similar results have also been reported that the incorporation of carbon dots can significantly reduce the microbial population of fruits during storage [11,39,59].

In this study, SA/Gel-based multifunctional films containing red pepper-based carbon dots (RP-CDs) were prepared. The effects of integration of 1, 2, and 3 wt% RP-CDs on the performance of SA/Gel films were explored. Microstructural and FT-IR analyses suggested uniform loading of RP-CDs within the polymer matrix through intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions. The SA/Gel/RP-CDs presented more favorable hydrophobicity, optical, and mechanical properties in an amount-dependent manner compared with pristine SA/Gel film. The addition of 3 wt% RP-CDs significantly improved the mechanical strength of the composite film by 53.0 %. In addition, it achieved a UV blocking rate of 99.1 %, an ABTS radical scavenging rate of 100 %, and a bactericidal efficacy of 99.9 % against L. monocytogenes. The SA/Gel/RP-CD3% active film was more effective in inhibiting microbial growth and preserving grape quality, extending the storage life to 24 days, compared to the other samples. Therefore, the experimental findings of the present work validate the potentiality of the proposed materials and pave the way to do further research with more precise parameters by following food safety standards. Overall, this study provides a feasible method for food waste recycling and opens up new possibilities for developing multifunctional nanomaterials from biowaste for active packaging applications.

Zohreh Riahi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ajahar Khan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology.
